# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about how Claude Code-generated pull requests were identified, sampled, accepted or rejected, revised, and categorized across open-source GitHub repositories, including comparative statistics against matched human-created pull requests.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Abstract; Introduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study analyzes 567 Claude Code-generated pull requests across 157 diverse open-source projects.

## Source Excerpt

“In this paper, we empirically study 567 GitHub pull requests (PRs) generated using Claude Code, an agentic coding tool, across 157 diverse open-source projects.”

## Note

Study scope is limited to the sampled PR set described in the paper.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Section 3, Data Collection
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Agentic PRs were identified by searching GitHub PR descriptions for the string “Generated with Claude Code.”

## Source Excerpt

“To identify these Agentic-PRs, we searched for those containing the comment string ‘Generated with Claude Code’ in their descriptions.”

## Note

This identification method captures explicitly labeled PRs rather than all possible Claude Code-assisted PRs.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Section 3, Data Collection
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The initial Agentic-PR search covered PRs submitted between February 24, 2025 and April 30, 2025 in repositories with at least 10 GitHub stars, yielding 797 PRs before filtering.

## Source Excerpt

“We conducted this search using the GitHub GraphQL API, focusing on PRs submitted between February 24, 2025 ... and April 30, 2025 ... To ensure relevance and quality, we limited this search to repositories with at least 10 GitHub stars. This search yielded 797 PRs.”

## Note

The paper treats this as the initial retrieval set before exclusion steps.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Section 3, Data Collection
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
After excluding 54 open PRs, the study retained 743 Agentic-PRs before constructing the matched comparison dataset.

## Source Excerpt

“We excluded 54 PRs that remained open (i.e., still under development). We identified 743 Agentic-PRs after filtering.”

## Note

This is an intermediate filtered set, not the final analysis set used for the matched agent-vs-human comparison.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: method
citation: Section 3, Data Collection
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The human comparison set was matched by author and repository, and 176 Agentic-PRs were excluded because a same-author human comparison set could not be obtained in a similar time frame.

## Source Excerpt

“Specifically, we selected PRs created during a similar time frame and by the same author, originating from the same project repositories... We excluded 176 Agentic-PRs from the agent-assisted coding dataset because we could not obtain the same number of Human-PRs created during a similar time frame and by the same author.”

## Note

The final paired dataset reflects this matching constraint rather than the full retrieved Agentic-PR population.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Table 2; Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: supports
---
475 of 567 Agentic-PRs were merged, corresponding to an 83.77425% acceptance rate.

## Source Excerpt

“Table 2 shows that 475 out of 567 APRs were merged... 83.77425% of Agentic-PRs are accepted by human reviewers.”

## Note

Acceptance is defined in the study as merged PRs.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Table 2; Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs was lower than for Human-PRs: 83.77425% versus 91.00529%, and the paper reports this difference as statistically significant.

## Source Excerpt

“83.77425% of Agentic-PRs are accepted by human reviewers, although their acceptance rate remains statistically lower than Human-PRs (91.00529%). A chi-squared test revealed that Human-PRs have a statistically significantly (p < 0.05) higher acceptance rate than Agentic-PRs.”

## Note

This evidence introduces a direct comparison showing lower acceptance for Agentic-PRs within the matched dataset.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: metric
citation: Table 2; Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Median time to merge was 1.23 hours for Agentic-PRs and 1.04 hours for Human-PRs, with no significant difference reported.

## Source Excerpt

“A log-rank test shows no significant difference in merge times between Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs... The median time to merge for APRs is 1.23 hours, while HPRs take 1.04 hours.”

## Note

The source reports similar review timing despite the lower acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: claim-support
citation: Table 3; Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Among rejected Agentic-PRs, 12.1% were rejected because another PR or developer implemented a different solution first.

## Source Excerpt

“Table 3 shows that alternative solutions (12.1%)... frequently result in Agentic-PR rejections,” and the table lists “Are implementated by other PRs/developers ... 12.1%.”

## Note

This is one named rejection category among rejected Agentic-PRs.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: claim-support
citation: Table 3; Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Among rejected Agentic-PRs, 5.5% were PRs created only to trigger automated verification and were not intended for merging.

## Source Excerpt

“Process-related issues such as verification-only submissions (5.5%)...” and the table lists “Submission for verification ... 5.5%.”

## Note

The paper treats these as workflow-related rejections rather than code-quality judgments.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: claim-support
citation: Table 3; Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
63.7% of rejected Agentic-PRs were closed without explanatory comments or discussion.

## Source Excerpt

“The majority (63.7%) of rejected Agentic-PRs were closed without explanatory comments or discussion. Table 3 shows that 58 out of 91 rejected APRs fell into this category.”

## Note

This leaves most rejection causes unobserved in the available review record.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: result
citation: Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Among merged PRs, 54.94737% of Agentic-PRs and 58.52713% of Human-PRs were merged without any revision, with no statistically significant difference reported.

## Source Excerpt

“Among merged PRs, we observe that 54.94737% of APRs (261 PRs) are merged as-is with a single commit, as compared to 58.52713% (302 PRs) of HPRs. In addition, the chi-squared test revealed no statistically significant difference between the two groups.”

## Note

This comparison concerns merged PRs only, not the full submitted set.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
When revisions were required, both Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs typically had a median of 2 revised commits before merge.

## Source Excerpt

“Analysis of revision patterns shows that both APRs and HPRs typically include a median of 2 revised commits prior to merge.”

## Note

The paper reports this as part of the revision-effort comparison.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: metric
citation: Section 4.1; Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Refactoring was more common in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs, at 24.9% versus 14.9% in the manually classified PR sample.

## Source Excerpt

“Code refactoring is more frequent in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs, accounting for 24.9% of Agentic-PRs compared to 14.9% of Human-PRs.”

## Note

These percentages come from the manually classified RQ1 sample.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Section 4.1; Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Test-related changes were more common in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs, at 18.8% versus 4.5% in the manually classified PR sample.

## Source Excerpt

“Test-related improvements are substantially more common in Agentic-PRs than in Human-PRs (18.8% versus 4.5%).”

## Note

These percentages are reported from the RQ1 classification sample.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-021
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: metric
citation: Section 4.4; Table 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.14745v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Among revised Agentic-PRs, the most common revision categories were bug fixes (45.1%), documentation updates (27.4%), refactoring (25.7%), and style changes (22.1%).

## Source Excerpt

“As shown in Table 4, the most common revision type was fixing functional bugs, accounting for 45.1%... Documentation updates were present in 27.4%... Refactoring is frequently required (25.7%)... 22.1% ... received style-related revisions during review.”

## Note

These categories describe the kinds of changes added during review after the initial agent-generated submission.
---

“We conducted this search using the GitHub GraphQL API, focusing on PRs submitted between February 24, 2025 ... and April 30, 2025 ... To ensure relevance and quality, we limited this search to repositories with at least 10 GitHub stars. This search yielded 797 PRs.”

## Note

The paper treats this as the initial retrieval set before exclusion steps.
```

### file: evidence-07.md

---
The acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs was lower than for Human-PRs: 83.77425% versus 91.00529%, and the paper reports this difference as statistically significant.

## Source Excerpt

“83.77425% of Agentic-PRs are accepted by human reviewers, although their acceptance rate remains statistically lower than Human-PRs (91.00529%). A chi-squared test revealed that Human-PRs have a statistically significantly (p < 0.05) higher acceptance rate than Agentic-PRs.”

## Note

This evidence introduces a direct comparison showing lower acceptance for Agentic-PRs within the matched dataset.
```

“As shown in Table 4, the most common revision type was fixing functional bugs, accounting for 45.1%... Documentation updates were present in 27.4%... Refactoring is frequently required (25.7%)... 22.1% ... received style-related revisions during review.”

These categories describe the kinds of changes added during review after the initial agent-generated submission.

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about how Claude Code-generated pull requests were identified, sampled, accepted or rejected, revised, and categorized across open-source GitHub repositories, including comparative statistics against matched human-created pull requests.
